meta.name: description
meta.content: Claude is Anthropic's AI, built for problem solvers. Tackle complex challenges, analyze data, write code, and think through your hardest work.

link.title: Winning hackathon strategy: theme and deployment roadmap - Claude

div.class: grid w-full overflow-hidden print:!h-auto print:overflow-visible print:block
div.style: height: calc(100dvh - var(--app-install-banner-height, 0px) - var(--dev-dashboard-height, 0px) - var(--desktop-org-banner-height, 0px) - var(--stale-session-redirect-guard-height, 0px) - var(--session-expiry-banner-height, 0px)); grid-template-rows: 0px 1fr; transition: grid-template-rows 0.15s ease-out;

div.p: Use the up and down arrow keys to move between messages.

button.class: sr-only select-none

div.h2: Claude responded: Just a few things to personalize before you submit — the structure and content are ready, but these specific spots need your input:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.span: Audited README sections for personalization and file verification

div.p: Just a few things to personalize before you submit — the structure and content are ready, but these specific spots need your input:
div.h3: 1. Add your team details (currently a placeholder)
div.p: At the very bottom, this line:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(234, 236, 240); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre-wrap;

span.span: *(Add your name and teammates' names here)*

div.p: Replace with your actual name(s), and your college/team name if your hackathon platform expects that.
div.h3: 2. Double-check the two image files are actually in your folder and named to match
div.p: The README references:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: architecture-diagram.svg (the one I made earlier in our chat — download it and place it in your main project folder)
confusion_matrix.png (generated when you ran train_model.py with the confusion matrix code)

div.p: Run this to confirm both exist:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

div.p: If either file is named differently on your computer (e.g., you saved the diagram with a different filename), either rename the file to match the README, or edit the README's references to match your actual filenames.
div.h3: 3. Verify the live link and repo link are still correct
div.p: At the very top:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

div.p: These match what you shared earlier — just confirm they're still the exact working URLs before submitting (copy-paste directly from your browser to avoid typos).
div.h3: 4. Optional — add your name as commit author if it isn't already
div.p: Not required for the README itself, but worth checking: if your hackathon platform looks at GitHub commit history/authorship, make sure your git commits show your name, not a generic one:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

div.h2: Claude responded: Good question to actually understand before you explain it to judges.

div.span: Calibrated explanation to balance accessibility with technical accuracy

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid grid-rows-[auto_auto] isolate min-w-0

div.p: Good question to actually understand before you explain it to judges. Let's break it down with your own project's example.

Computers can't read words — they only understand numbers. So before your model can learn from messages like "building collapsed people trapped inside", that sentence needs to become a list of numbers somehow. TF-IDF is one way to do that conversion — and a smart one, because it doesn't just count words, it figures out which words actually matter.
div.h3: Breaking down the name: TF × IDF
div.p: TF = Term Frequency — "how often does this word appear in this one message?"
Simple idea: if the word "fire" appears 3 times in a message, that message is probably really about fire. More repeats of a word = probably more important to that specific message.
IDF = Inverse Document Frequency — "how rare is this word across ALL messages?"
This is the clever part. Imagine two words appear in your dataset:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: The word "the" — appears in almost every single message (because it's just a common English word)
The word "trapped" — appears in only a small number of messages, specifically the emergency ones

div.p: TF-IDF says: "the" is common everywhere, so it's not useful for telling messages apart — give it a low score. But "trapped" is rare, appearing mainly in genuinely urgent messages — so when it does show up, it's a strong, meaningful signal — give it a high score.

TF-IDF multiplies these two ideas: "how often does this word show up in this message" × "how rare/meaningful is this word overall." A word that appears often in one message AND is rare across the whole dataset gets the highest score — meaning it's a genuinely distinctive, important word for that message.
div.h3: Concrete example from your own project
div.p: Take: "fire spreading fast in my building need help now"

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Words like "in," "my," "now" → appear in tons of messages regardless of severity → low TF-IDF score, model learns to mostly ignore them
Words like "fire," "spreading," "help" → appear far less often overall, but strongly associated with urgent messages when they do appear → high TF-IDF score, model pays close attention to these

div.p: So instead of your model treating every word equally, TF-IDF automatically highlights the words that actually distinguish "this is an emergency" from "this is just a normal sentence" — and those highlighted numbers are what actually get fed into your Logistic Regression model to learn from.
div.h3: One sentence for your pitch, if a judge asks

p.em: "TF-IDF converts each message into numbers by scoring words based on how often they appear in that message versus how rare they are across all messages — so common filler words get ignored, and distinctive emergency words like 'trapped' or 'bleeding' get weighted heavily, which is what our model actually learns from."

div.h2: Claude responded: "Triage" is a word from emergency medicine, and it's the exact concept your whole project is built around — worth knowing precisely since you'll likely say it …

div.p: "Triage" is a word from emergency medicine, and it's the exact concept your whole project is built around — worth knowing precisely since you'll likely say it out loud several times in your pitch.

p.strong: Triage = sorting people or situations by urgency, to decide who gets help first.

div.p: The word actually comes from the French word trier, meaning "to sort" — originally used by battlefield doctors who had too many wounded soldiers and too few doctors, and had to quickly decide: who needs help right now to survive, who can safely wait, and who's beyond saving. That sorting process is triage.
div.h3: How it applies today, in a hospital
div.p: Walk into any busy emergency room and you'll see triage in action: a nurse briefly checks each incoming patient and assigns them a priority — not first-come-first-served, but most-urgent-first. Someone having a heart attack gets seen before someone with a mild sprained ankle, even if the ankle patient arrived first. That quick sorting decision is triage.
div.h3: How it applies to your project
div.p: This is exactly what your AI model does, just applied to emergency reports instead of patients standing in a hospital line:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: A message saying "small pothole on the road" → low priority, can wait
A message saying "building collapsed, people trapped" → critical priority, needs help immediately

div.p: Your model reads each incoming report and instantly sorts it into critical / medium / low — that sorting decision is the triage. Traditionally, a human dispatcher does this sorting by ear, one call at a time. Your project automates that exact sorting step.
div.h3: One sentence to use in your pitch

p.em: "Triage means sorting emergencies by how urgent they are, so the most critical cases get help first — that's literally what our model does automatically for every incoming report, the same job a hospital nurse or 112 dispatcher does manually today."

div.p: This is actually a great word to lean on in your video intro, since it instantly signals to judges "this person understands the real-world emergency response domain," not just "this person built an app."

div.p: Here it is again, ready to download:

div.p: Download this and save it directly into your main emergency triage project folder (same level as app.py).
div.h3: 2. confusion_matrix.png — I genuinely cannot recreate this one, and here's why that matters
div.p: This file isn't something I made — it's a graph generated from your own trained model's actual predictions on your actual test data. I don't have access to your training_data.csv, your specific train/test split, or your model's real output — so if I generated a fake confusion matrix image right now, it would be showing made-up numbers, not your real model's real performance. That would be dishonest in your submission, and exactly the kind of thing that falls apart if a judge asks a follow-up question about it.

p.strong: The real fix: check why it didn't save on your computer, and generate it properly.

div.p: Run this to check if train_model.py actually has the confusion matrix code in it:
